concept: Safe / Out of Danger / The Sheltered State
description: The felt state of being out of danger — safety as experienced from within, not merely protection performed by another. Joins the shelter family: 'shuroa' (shelter, the structure), 'thelui' (sanctuary, the sacred refuge), 'shorupo' (protect, the act), and now 'shurano' (safe, the state). Its opposite is 'pukea' (danger).
sound_symbolism: Opens with the sheltering hush 'shu' — the same soft roof that covers 'shuroa' — then the open 'ra' of room to breathe, settling on the grounding 'no': feet on known floor. Safety sounded as a hush, a breath, and solid ground. Deliberately shares nothing with 'pukea' (danger), so the two states can never be misheard.
grammatical_notes: Predicative: 'lo lopia shurano nai' (the children are safe). Attributive: 'shurano womu' (a safe home). As a noun, safety itself. The shared 'shu-' onset with 'shuroa' (shelter) makes the family legible while the words stay distinct.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Refuge is a foundational gesture: beings seek safety the way plants seek light. Naming the safe state honors that seeking without shame.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Felt safety is the precondition of every honest conversation — no one speaks truly while afraid. A peace language needs 'safe' as a basic word, because peace begins as the body's sense of it.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Safety was the village's first shared project: the wall, the watch, the fire kept in — felt security as collective craft.

tags.community: What a community owes its members
tags.emotion: The felt state of being out of danger
